title: Vercel Infrastructure Breach via Compromised Context.ai Third-Party AI Tool — ShinyHunters Claims $2M Ransom, Customer Env Vars Exposed
coverage_detail: No existing Vectimus Cedar policy addresses this incident. The attack vector — endpoint malware infecting a third-party AI vendor's employee, stealing Google Workspace credentials, and abusing an OAuth federation integration — operates entirely outside the AI agent tool-call boundary where Vectimus enforces. No policy in the secrets, supchain, infra, or agentgov packs intercepts OAuth identity federation or endpoint credential theft at a third-party vendor.
gap_description: The root cause is a traditional supply chain credential compromise: Lumma Stealer malware on a Context.ai employee endpoint harvested Google Workspace credentials and API keys, which were then used to traverse a Google Workspace OAuth integration into Vercel's infrastructure. Vectimus Cedar evaluates policies only at AI agent tool-call time (shell_command, file_read, file_write, web_fetch, mcp_tool_call, git_operation, message_send). The attack chain contains no AI agent tool calls — it is entirely a human endpoint compromise → stolen identity → OAuth federation abuse sequence at the cloud infrastructure layer. This gap cannot be closed by any Cedar policy; it requires vendor security vetting, OAuth permission scoping (principle of least privilege on third-party integrations), mandatory MFA for service accounts, and endpoint security controls at the third-party vendor. No new Vectimus policy is appropriate.

sources.title: Vercel Breach Tied to Context.ai Hack — The Hacker News
Hack at Vercel Sends Crypto Developers Scrambling to Lock Down API Keys — CoinDesk
Vercel Data Breach Linked to Context.ai Hack — Technadu
Vercel Breached, Hacker Demanding $2M Ransom — NewsBytesApp

summary: On 19 April 2026, Vercel disclosed that attackers breached its infrastructure by pivoting through a compromised employee account at Context.ai, a third-party AI analytics tool with a Google Workspace OAuth integration into Vercel environments. A Context.ai employee had been infected with Lumma Stealer malware in February 2026 after downloading malicious files disguised as Roblox exploits; the malware harvested Google Workspace credentials and API keys. Attackers — linked to ShinyHunters — used these credentials to access Vercel systems, exposing non-sensitive customer environment variables and claiming 580 employee records. A $2M ransom was demanded and a global supply chain attack threatened. Vercel engaged Mandiant and law enforcement and urged all customers to rotate credentials. The attack chain is entirely outside the Vectimus tool-call boundary: no AI coding agent tool calls were involved. The breach illustrates how AI analytics vendors with broad OAuth access to developer infrastructure represent a high-value, under-scrutinised supply chain target.

title: FastGPT CVE-2026-40351 / CVE-2026-40352: NoSQL Injection Enables Unauthenticated Admin Takeover of AI Agent Building Platform
coverage_detail: No existing Vectimus Cedar policy covers server-side NoSQL injection in AI platform authentication endpoints. The attack (injecting MongoDB query operators such as {"$ne": ""} into the FastGPT login or password-change HTTP endpoint) occurs entirely at the application server layer via a direct HTTP request. Vectimus evaluates Cedar policies at agent tool-call time via pretool hooks; it has no visibility into FastGPT's inbound HTTP traffic. The supply chain pack (SUPPLY-001) only blocks pinned installations of known-vulnerable Python packages during agent shell or package commands — it does not cover web application authentication flaws in platforms agents are built on. The database safety pack (vectimus-db-001 through vectimus-db-007) covers ORM destructive-flag abuse, not injection into authentication layers. No policy in any of the 11 packs addresses this attack vector, and none can: the exploit fires on the FastGPT server before any AI agent tool call is involved.
gap_description: The root cause — missing runtime type validation on MongoDB query input in FastGPT's authentication and password-change API endpoints — exists entirely within the FastGPT server application. The exploit is a conventional server-side NoSQL injection (CWE-943) delivered over HTTP with no agent tool call in the path. Vectimus's enforcement boundary is the tool-call layer inside AI coding agents (Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot); it cannot intercept or inspect inbound HTTP requests to a separately deployed web platform. Mitigation requires upgrading FastGPT to v4.14.9.5 or later, enforcing strict type-checking on all query input parameters at the application level, and restricting FastGPT admin interfaces to trusted network segments.

sources.title: FastGPT Security Advisory GHSA-x8mx-2mr7-h9xg: NoSQL Injection in Authentication and Password Change Endpoints
NVD: CVE-2026-40351 — FastGPT Unauthenticated MongoDB Operator Injection (CVSS 9.8)
FastGPT v4.14.9.5 Release Notes — Patches CVE-2026-40351 and CVE-2026-40352

summary: Two NoSQL injection vulnerabilities published 17 April 2026 affect FastGPT (labring/FastGPT), an open-source AI agent building platform. CVE-2026-40351 (CVSS 9.8, Critical) allows an unauthenticated attacker to bypass password-based login entirely by injecting a MongoDB query operator (e.g. {"$ne": ""}) into the password field, granting root administrator access with no credentials. CVE-2026-40352 (CVSS 8.8, High) allows any authenticated low-privilege user to hijack any other account by injecting MongoDB operators into the old-password field of the password-change endpoint, bypassing the ownership check. An attacker who exploits CVE-2026-40351 gains full administrative control over the FastGPT instance and all AI agents deployed on it. Both vulnerabilities stem from missing runtime type validation on user-supplied JSON fields before they are passed to MongoDB. Fixed in FastGPT v4.14.9.5 (GHSA-x8mx-2mr7-h9xg). The attack vector is direct HTTP to the FastGPT server; no AI agent tool call is involved and the incident is entirely outside Vectimus's tool-call enforcement boundary.
